stem: A random sample of five observations, \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, [[x5]] \} \), has sample mean \( [[mbar]] \). What is the sample variance (the unbiased estimate that divides by \( n-1 \))?
solution: The unbiased sample variance divides the summed squared deviations from \( \bar{x} = [[mbar]] \) by \( n-1 = 4 \): \( s^2 = \frac{[[ssq]]}{4} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( n = 5 \); that is the (biased) population variance, not the unbiased sample variance.
This is the total of the squared deviations without dividing at all.
This divides by \( n-2 = 3 \); the unbiased divisor is \( n-1 \), not \( n-2 \).
This is off by one from the correct sample variance.

stem: A random sample of five observations, \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, [[x5]] \} \), has sample mean \( [[x3]] \). What is the sample standard deviation (the square root of the unbiased variance, dividing by \( n-1 \))?
solution: Take the square root of the unbiased sample variance: \( s = \sqrt{\dfrac{(-2[[k]])^2+(-2[[k]])^2+0^2+(2[[k]])^2+(2[[k]])^2}{4}} = \sqrt{4[[k]]^2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( n=5 \) inside the square root; that gives the population standard deviation, not the sample one.
This is the sample variance itself, without taking the square root.
This takes the square root of the sum of squared VALUES (not deviations from the mean) before dividing.
This is off by one from the correct sample standard deviation.
This is off by one from the correct sample standard deviation.

stem: For a sample of \( [[n]] \) observations, the biased (population-formula) variance — dividing the summed squared deviations by \( n \) — is \( [[V]] \). What is the unbiased sample variance (dividing that same sum by \( n-1 \) instead)?
solution: Both formulas share the same sum of squared deviations, \( n\cdot[[V]] \), so the unbiased variance is \( \dfrac{n\cdot[[V]]}{n-1} = \dfrac{[[n]]\cdot[[V]]}{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the biased and unbiased variances are always equal.
This inverts the adjustment ratio, multiplying by \( (n-1)/n \) instead of \( n/(n-1) \).
This divides the biased variance by \( n \) again instead of rescaling by \( n/(n-1) \).
This multiplies the biased variance by \( n \) without dividing by \( n-1 \) at all.
This is off by one from the correct unbiased variance.

stem: A sample has sum of squared deviations from the mean equal to \( [[S]] \). If the unbiased sample variance (dividing by \( n-1 \)) is \( [[V]] \), what is the sample size \( n \)?
solution: Since \( [[V]] = \dfrac{[[S]]}{n-1} \), solving for \( n \) gives \( n = \dfrac{[[S]]}{[[V]]} + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This finds \( n-1 \) but forgets to add 1 to get \( n \) itself.
This subtracts 1 instead of adding it, moving in the wrong direction from \( n-1 \).
This inverts the ratio, dividing \( [[V]] \) by \( [[S]] \) instead of the other way around.
This multiplies the sum of squares by the variance instead of dividing.
This is off by one from the correct sample size.
